rationale: Detects unusual AI behavior and performance shifts; maps systems to risks and measures performance.
Real-time monitoring of AI model performance, drift, and bias; tracks identified risks over time.
Identifies AI-native indicators of compromise (e.g., adversarial prompts); maps system context to risks.
Ensures rapid identification and recovery from AI-related incidents to reduce impact and recurrence.
Independent testing of digital and AI environments to validate control effectiveness.
Integrates AI-specific threat intelligence to anticipate evolving adversarial AI tactics.
Visible leadership commitment and resource allocation for AI governance and oversight.
Formal independent oversight bodies for AI governance and ethical decision-making.
Strategic direction and board-level independent oversight of the AI management system.
Ensures traceable human ownership and authority to act on AI risk findings.
Adopts quantitative fairness metrics and mitigates disparities in AI system outcomes.
Promotes inclusive data practices and diverse stakeholder engagement for AI systems.
Respects cultural norms and jurisdictional sensitivities in AI outputs and labeling.
Documents and justifies trade-offs between AI accuracy, privacy, and fairness.
Publishes explainability statements and discloses AI use to users and regulators.
Defines permissible and prohibited behaviors for personnel using AI technologies.
Establishes a culture of responsible data and AI use throughout the development lifecycle.
Operationalizes human-centric AI principles like fairness and explainability.
Hierarchical policy architecture covering security, privacy, and AI governance.
Enforceable security expectations to protect AI confidentiality, integrity, and availability.
Preparedness and rapid recovery policies for AI-specific failure modes.
Principle-based rules for responsible and secure AI design and deployment.
Rules for fair processing of personal data aligned to AI system needs.
Promotes human-centric technology use through enforceable ethical standards.
Ensures personnel practices support secure and ethical AI-related conduct.
Standards for suppliers and partners providing AI software or datasets.
Ensures data quality, integrity, and lawful provenance for AI training.
Operationalizes policy commitments into measurable and auditable technical standards.
Technical standards for system hardening and AI-specific infrastructure security.
Standardized processes for AI failure recovery and business continuity.
Detailed standards for data, model testing, and deployment monitoring.
Compliance with data protection laws through demonstrable oversight.
Protection of proprietary algorithms, models, and training datasets.
Conformance to legal obligations for AI transparency and safety.
Alignment of cybersecurity controls with contractual and legal obligations.
Ensures AI practices are non-discriminatory and ethically reviewed.
Embeds ethical tenets into the design and deployment of digital systems.
Lawful and ethical handling of sensitive data across AI operations.
Unified framework integrating AI, security, and operational risks.
Approved thresholds for acceptable AI and technical risks.
AI-specific risk management across the entire system lifecycle.
Analyzes cybersecurity risks aligned to threat intelligence and AI impact.
Identifies and manages risks from third-party AI software and data.
Precise technical boundaries to ensure controls are correctly audited.
Aligns security and AI governance with the organization’s purpose.
Risk-informed governance goals based on internal and external context.
Identification and risk categorization of all AI systems under control.
Proportionate protection of assets based on criticality and risk.
Addresses applicable legal and customer requirements for AI.
Living inventory of all AI components and system metadata.
Evaluates potential AI impacts on individuals and society.
Systematically identifies AI, privacy, and safety risks.
Mitigates risks from insiders who may compromise AI data or models.
Manages risks from software, model, and dataset suppliers.
Defines measurable thresholds for drift, fairness, and safety.
Tracks threats to AI assets to inform defensive investment.
Embeds TEVV and governance from design to decommissioning.
Structured, risk-informed reporting for AI governance committees.
Independent assurance of AI risk controls and lifecycle evidence.
Safe channels for reporting AI ethical or safety concerns.
Independent assessment of AI governance and TEVV evidence.
Executes security and reliability tests for AI components.
Evaluates model performance and harms across operating contexts.
Verifies that systems meet design and policy requirements.
Confirms the system achieves intended use without unacceptable risk.
